skills_i_can_now_do: Write an arguable claim with a side + reason ('should ___ because ___'), both with a frame and without one
Tell apart a fact, a bare opinion, and a real claim
Apply the 3-question test (takes a side? could someone disagree? gives a reason?) to check a claim

open_questions: When does a link count as cause vs. sequence? Taking in CO2 happens before making sugar (sequence) but the sugar also depends on it (cause) — how do I tell which is the truer relationship when both fit?

In Step 8 I went against the instructions and used 'as a result' instead of 'then' because splitting water causes the oxygen release — was I right to override, or does 'then' work if my science reading is off?

skills_i_can_now_do: Spot a vague back-reference — a bare 'this'/'it'/'that' the reader can't pin to one clear thing
Fix a vague reference by adding a noun after 'this'/'that' that names the actual thing meant (not just any noun)
Recognize that adding a noun isn't enough if the noun is still vague (like 'This problem' when there are multiple problems)
Tell the difference between anchoring a reference with a short naming phrase vs. clunkily repeating the whole previous sentence
Spot the trap where fixing pronoun number (singular vs plural) is grammar-correct but still reference-vague
Apply the 3-question reference checklist (back-reference? points to one clear thing? if not, add a noun)
Rewrite a vague-reference sentence with the frame, naming the combined idea (e.g. 'This set of challenges — finding food, dodging storms, and crossing open water —')
Invent my own anchoring noun cold, without a frame, avoiding a noun that repeats a word already in the sentence (e.g. 'That three-step routine' instead of 'That preparation... prepare')
Name three listed things as one combined idea with a short noun phrase (e.g. 'This triple threat' for ash, gas, and lava)
Transfer the reference-anchoring move to a brand-new topic (volcano source)
terms_learned: anchoring a reference (adding a noun after 'this'/'that' to name the thing)
where_i_struggled: Step 6 option (D) tricked me for a couple seconds — matching the pronoun to plural number ('They') FELT like a real grammar fix, but grammar-correct can still be reference-vague
Step 11: my first rewrite repeated the whole list ('This burst of ash, gas, and lava') and I had to think about whether that was the Step 5 (B) mistake before landing on a shorter naming phrase
Step 9 took actual thinking because the obvious noun ('preparation') would sound ridiculous next to 'prepare' — I had to invent a different noun
Step 8 ran the entire check for me in the prompt and even suggested the noun, so my job felt like copying — same complaint as the last lesson; I'd rather come up with the noun myself
open_questions: Is a shorter fix okay when the things are named in the previous sentence (like 'This set of challenges is what makes migration so hard'), or should I always name them out fully like the Step 3 example did?
When does 'this + noun' plus re-listing the items count as too much repeating (the Step 5 (B) mistake) vs. an acceptable full naming?

skills_i_can_now_do: Combine the transitions skill (lesson 13) and the anchored-reference skill (lesson 14) in one revision pass — fixing filler 'also's AND floating 'this'/'it' in the same paragraph
Apply the read → name → fix → confirm revision routine to a choppy paragraph
Pick out the cohered version of a paragraph in multiple choice by spotting real transitions (add, cause) and an anchored 'this'
Diagnose what a choppy paragraph needs, including catching a contrast relationship hiding behind an 'also' on my own
Name the relationships between sentences (add, contrast, cause, conclusion) before revising
Revise an all-benefits paragraph with add + add transitions and an anchored concluding 'this'
Rewrite a contrast hidden behind 'also' using 'however'/'but' and anchor the floating 'this' with a specific noun phrase (like 'this tradeoff')

Do the full revision without the checklist: find the hidden contrast myself, name add/contrast links, and anchor the final 'this' with a clear noun phrase (like 'this clash between benefits and cost')
Transfer the revision move to a new topic (community service paragraph) with the same structure — add, contrast, anchored reference, conclusion
terms_learned: read, name, fix, confirm (the revision routine)
where_i_struggled: Steps 2 and 9 told me to decide a side and pick a reason, but I never used it anywhere — the writing steps just handed me paragraphs to revise, so the side-picking felt pointless and confusing

This is now the FIFTH checklist/tool in the course (claim test, strength test, explain test, 'the 3 parts', now 'read, name, fix, confirm') and I'm the one keeping them all straight in my head — it's getting to be a lot
Steps 4 and 5 tested basically the same skill from two directions ('which one is cohered' vs 'what does this one need') — same doubling as the warrant lesson
Step 6's paragraph had no hidden contrast, so it was basically the Step 3 example with different nouns — I mostly copied the pattern from the AFTER

Step 7 ran the check FOR me — the lesson told me the contrast was there and even handed me the word 'tradeoff', so I was just assembling their answer instead of checking myself (same problem as lesson 9)
Step 8's directions hinted 'watch for one link that is really a contrast', which gave away the trap before I could find it — the course keeps telling me where the trap is
Step 10's paragraph had the exact same skeleton as Step 8's (benefit, benefit, contrast, floating 'this'), so it felt like tracing the same shape with new words, not a real transfer test
open_questions: Why do the source/debate steps (Steps 2 and 9) ask me to decide a side and pick a reason when I never write my own claim or use it in the revision steps?
Would I have caught the contrast in Step 7 on my own if the lesson hadn't already told me? (I did catch one in Step 5, so maybe — but the step never let me try.)

skills_i_can_now_do: Spot the three informal-tone markers in a sentence: first person ('I think,' 'I feel like,' 'in my opinion'), slang/casual words ('totally,' 'honestly,' 'awesome,' 'a ton more'), and reader-address ('you')
Rewrite an informal claim into formal tone while keeping the position and reason exactly the same, both with the frame and without it (cold)
Apply the 3-question tone check (first person cut? slang cut? talking to the reader?) to test whether a rewrite is formal
Avoid the 'thin claim' mistake from the worked example — deleting the reason along with the casual language (I caught myself almost doing this with 'clearly' in Step 8)
Spot a 'formal-sounding fact' trap in multiple choice — a sentence that has clean tone but drops the position (connected it to lesson 1's fact-dressed-as-claim trap)
Do the formal-tone rewrite on the anti side of a debate, not just the side I'd pick (five-day week rewrite in Step 9)
Transfer the formal-tone rewrite to a new topic without the frame (free transit claim in Step 11)
Translate casual words into formal equivalents while keeping the meaning ('kids' → 'students,' 'awesome' → 'benefit,' 'a ton more' → 'significantly increase')

Step 8 ran the whole 3-question check FOR me (printed the diagnosis before I answered), so there was nothing left to actually check — same pattern as lesson 10 step 8 and lesson 15 step 7

When a source has more topics than the task asks about, how do I know for sure which ones to leave out?

felt_repeated.what: The gate felt almost exactly like lesson 24 (writing a full informational essay) and lesson 25 (revising one) — no new move, just everything at once on my own, so it felt like a test on stuff already done once rather than something new

skills_i_can_now_do: Write a full argument essay on an unseen source in one sitting, hitting the whole gate checklist (thesis with a side, reasons with attributed evidence, counterclaim named and answered, conclusion that lands the upshot)
Spot a statistics trap in a source — noticing that percentages of different pools (41% vs 42%) can't be compared, and using the gallons-per-day numbers instead
Identify the strongest fact in a source (the hidden dependency: irrigation runs on electric pumps) and build the thesis around it
Plan an essay before drafting: thesis, two reasons with matched evidence, counterclaim + answer, and conclusion upshot
Name the counterclaim's best point fairly and answer it in a way that concedes something (power-first can't mean farms get zero)
Use attributive tags for evidence ('According to the passage', 'The source, citing the U.S. Geological Survey')
Write a conclusion that lands an upshot (which to save first so both survive) instead of restating the thesis
Self-score the essay against the gate checklist
where_i_struggled: Middle school habit still fights the upshot conclusion — my brain wants to write 'In conclusion, power should get the water first' and stop

The source's last paragraph suggests the essay's direction (like 'a rule for rationing in the worst years'), and I'm still not sure if using that idea makes the upshot mine or just borrowing the source's vibe — same uncertainty from earlier lessons about where the line is between my wording and the source's
open_questions: Where is the line between saying a source's suggested idea in my own words vs. borrowing the source's vibe — when does an upshot count as MINE?
Is this lesson a second gate on an unseen source, and why does it come right after lesson 26's gate?

felt_repeated.what: Student said this lesson felt like a repeat of lesson 23 (full argument essay) and lesson 26 (the gate essay) — no new skills, just doing everything at once on a new unseen source
